title: Omphalocele & Gastroschisis: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of Omphalocele & Gastroschisis
Identify key diagnostic features of Omphalocele & Gastroschisis
Apply clinical reasoning to Omphalocele & Gastroschisis management
Recognize common pitfalls in Omphalocele & Gastroschisis

sections.title: Overview of Omphalocele & Gastroschisis
sections.content:
**Omphalocele & Gastroschisis**

Lateral fold closure defects vs vascular disruption, peritoneal covering difference, associated anomalies

**Key Points:**
• Omphalocele: midline, covered by peritoneal sac, contains bowel ± liver, many associated anomalies (trisomies, Beckwith-Wiedemann)
• Gastroschisis: right of umbilicus, NO peritoneal covering, bowel directly exposed, few associated anomalies
• Omphalocele: failure of lateral body folds to close

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• Omphalocele: midline, covered by peritoneal sac, contains bowel ± liver, many associated anomalies (trisomies, Beckwith-Wiedemann)
• Gastroschisis: right of umbilicus, NO peritoneal covering, bowel directly exposed, few associated anomalies

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• Omphalocele: failure of lateral body folds to close
• Gastroschisis: likely vascular disruption of right omphalomesenteric artery
• Gastroschisis needs immediate surgical coverage (silo/primary closure); Omphalocele may use delayed closure if sac intact

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Omphalocele & Gastroschisis:**

• Omphalocele: failure of lateral body folds to close
• Gastroschisis: likely vascular disruption of right omphalomesenteric artery
• Gastroschisis needs immediate surgical coverage (silo/primary closure); Omphalocele may use delayed closure if sac intact
